title: Podman rootless containers not accessible on the network, following a reboot, until containers are restarted.
body:
Hi, 

I have an issue where currently I am running 2 rootless  containers via quadlets ( Ejabberd and Jellyfin) on a Fedora CoreOS server and after a host reboot, I cant access the containers although they have started correctly as seen by the logs.

The rootful containers (cockpit and adguard) start and are accessible with out issue. After everything has started I have to restart the rootless containers to make them accessible again.

I've tried everything I can think of to discover the reason for this issue.

Server: Fedora CoreOS 43.20260105.3.0
podman version 5.7.1

Pastebin of log from when network-online.target is reached until the containers are restarted

https://bin.2255.me/?4a6dd3a3aeaa79d3#ALRARGwfRVRW8kiuQNBk5LxD7DxgMsxXRj8vrNPBUrfj

this is  the quadlet for the Jellyfin if it helps

```
[Unit]
Description=jellyfin
After=adguardhome.service

[Container]
ContainerName=jellyfin
Image=docker.io/jellyfin/jellyfin:latest
AutoUpdate=registry
PublishPort=8096:8096/tcp
UserNS=keep-id
AddDevice=/dev/dri/:/dev/dri/
Volume=/var/home/core/jellyfin/config:/config:Z
Volume=/var/home/core/jellyfin/cache:/cache:Z
Volume=/var/mydrive/storage/media:/media:Z

[Service]
# Inform systemd of additional exit status
SuccessExitStatus=0 143

[Install]
# Start by default on boot
WantedBy=default.target
```

is there something I'm missing or is this an issue with pasta ?

comments.body:
Can you run these commands and provide the output here when you have no connectivity (replace `<name>` with the name of the container)
```
# to see the container addresses
podman unshare nsenter -n$(podman inspect --format '{{.NetworkSettings.SandboxKey}}' <name>) ip addr
podman unshare nsenter -n$(podman inspect --format '{{.NetworkSettings.SandboxKey}}' <name>) ip route
podman unshare nsenter -n$(podman inspect --format '{{.NetworkSettings.SandboxKey}}' <name>) ip -6 route
# for the host addresses
ip addr
ip route
ip -6 route
``` 

Most likely what it means the container start up before the network is fully configured and/or pasta has picks the wrong interface/routes because of that.

this is for the jellyfin container  after reboot 

**podman unshare nsenter -n$(podman inspect --format '{{.NetworkSettings.SandboxKey}}' <name>) ip addr**
```
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host proto kernel_lo 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: podman0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65520 qdisc fq_codel state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/ether f6:ad:38:3d:ce:fe brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 10.88.0.1/16 brd 10.88.255.255 scope global podman0
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::f4ad:38ff:fe3d:cefe/64 scope link nodad proto kernel_ll 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
```
**podman unshare nsenter -n$(podman inspect --format '{{.NetworkSettings.SandboxKey}}' <name>) ip route**

```
       
10.88.0.0/16 dev podman0 proto kernel scope link 
10.88.0.0/16 dev podman0 proto kernel scope link src 10.88.0.1 
```
**podman unshare nsenter -n$(podman inspect --format '{{.NetworkSettings.SandboxKey}}' <name>) ip -6 route**

```

fe80::/64 dev podman0 proto kernel metric 256 pref medium
default via fe80::1 dev podman0 metric 1024 pref medium
```

**ip addr**
```
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host noprefixroute 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: eno1: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc mq state UP group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 78:55:36:05:83:55 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    altname enp2s0
    altname enx785536058355
    inet 192.168.1.250/24 brd 192.168.1.255 scope global noprefixroute eno1
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::3dfe:63ea:d606:b92b/64 scope link noprefixroute 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
3: podman0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state UP group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 8e:49:0a:bc:88:c5 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 10.88.0.1/16 brd 10.88.255.255 scope global podman0
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::8c49:aff:febc:88c5/64 scope link proto kernel_ll 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
4: veth0@if2: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue master podman0 state UP group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 92:e3:2e:d5:d4:72 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff link-netns netns-6587bd1a-a885-29cd-d401-8a9192564ed6
    inet6 fe80::8d4:bdff:fe2f:755c/64 scope link proto kernel_ll 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
5: wg0: <POINTOPOINT,NOARP,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1420 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/none 
    inet 10.100.0.1/24 scope global wg0
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
```
**ip route**

```
default via 192.168.1.1 dev eno1 proto dhcp src 192.168.1.250 metric 100 
10.88.0.0/16 dev podman0 proto kernel scope link src 10.88.0.1 
10.100.0.0/24 dev wg0 proto kernel scope link src 10.100.0.1 
192.168.1.0/24 dev eno1 proto kernel scope link src 192.168.1.250 metric 100 
```

**ip -6 route**

```
fe80::/64 dev podman0 proto kernel metric 256 pref medium
fe80::/64 dev veth0 proto kernel metric 256 pref medium
fe80::/64 dev eno1 proto kernel metric 1024 pref medium
```


it's exactly the same for the ejabberd pod also

Ack this does sounds like the typical timing issue then of pasta starting before your host networking is fully ready so the network-online.target is likely finished despite there not being a proper route yet I guess.

There isn't really anything we can do about it today, I see you commented already on https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/24796#issuecomment-3817279540 and that is basically it as network-online is not reliable I guess.
There we mention the possible network monitor feature of pasta which then would just update the routes/address inside the container as the host  which should resolve the start to early problems and some others when the address changes later.

I guess as work around you can try to modify the podman-user-wait-network-online.service yourself with a better command that checks it according to your env (`ip -o route | grep default` maybe?) or well the lazy fix add some `sleep 10` there or something if you are fine with it.

If you want to be sure this is the problem you could add this to the quadlet file

```
[Service]
ExecStartPre=/usr/bin/ip route
```

And the check the logs how the routes looked like when the unit was started, that should confirm that it is not yet fully set up.

But yeah I would say I close this as dup because the fix is the same https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/24796

Ah ,  I suspected it may have been a similar issue. I would try the option of editing podman-user-wait-network-online.service, but its located in **/usr/lib/systemd/user/** which is not an writable directory in fedora CoreOS being atomic . i think I may go the root of swapping to rootful containers and utilise **userns=auto**  to resolve this issue until a fix comes along. 
Thankyou for debugging my issue though.

> I would try the option of editing podman-user-wait-network-online.service, but its located in /usr/lib/systemd/user/ which is not an writable directory in fedora CoreOS being atomic 

That is not a problem, systemd files follow a well defined hierarchy, you can replace the file by putting it in `/etc/systemd/user` or even your home dir then it is just changed for the one user, i.e. `/home/<username>/.config/systemd/user/`

You can also create drop in files there to override only certain fields, an easy way is via `systemctl --user  edit podman-user-wait-network-online.service`

your a legend this worked a charm , I went the lazy route unfortunately and added  


```
[Service]
ExecStart=
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c 'until systemctl is-active network-online.target; do sleep 10; done'
```
using **systemctl --user  edit podman-user-wait-network-online.service** 

the containers start as expected and are available to the network without a restart.
